title: Beachhead Strategy

content.statement: Dominate one narrow segment before expanding.
content.question: Have I defined a segment small enough that I could realistically own a majority of it?
content.equation: Beachhead Strength = (Segment Share + Reference Density + Whole-Product Fit) ÷ 3
content.mental_model: Beachhead, secure one strip of sand before pushing inland.
content.business_lens: Pick a segment small enough to dominate; aim for 50%+ share; expand from references.
content.life_lens: Focus on a small known audience; serve them obsessively before broadening.
sop: Tailor product, pricing, marketing exclusively to it
Drive toward majority share within the segment
Use references to enter adjacent segments
content.explainer: Salesforce dominated SMB tech CRM before moving up-market. Tesla started with high-end Roadster buyers. The pattern: a beachhead small enough to win, defensible enough to provide a base, similar enough to the next segment that references travel.
questions: Have I defined a segment small enough that I could realistically own a majority of it?
If someone asked you to name your ideal customer in one sentence, would that description fit 50 people or 5,000?
What would have to be true for you to say you dominate your current segment, and how far are you from that number today?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Segment Share 6, Reference Density 4, Whole-Product Fit 7. Score = (6 + 4 + 7) ÷ 3 = 5.7. Higher means you're close to genuinely owning your chosen niche; lower means you're still spread thin inside your own beachhead.
content.closing_statement: Owning a small market beats renting a big one.

caption:
Principle Nº 292: Beachhead Strategy

Dominate one narrow segment before expanding.

Ask yourself: Have I defined a segment small enough that I could realistically own a majority of it?
